Key Vault protections are strong tenant-wide (enablepurgeprotection True for 100% and soft delete retention 90 days for ~94% of vaults): because the property aggregations show purge protection and long soft-delete retention as defaults, you should store DB credentials and high-value secrets in Key Vault and design the finance app to use purge-protected vaults with soft-delete rather than ad-hoc secret storage.

Managed identities are widely adopted (370 user-assigned identities tenant-wide and examples like VMs showing SystemAssigned,UserAssigned in ~98.8% of cases): since identity-based access is an established convention, plan to use system- or user-assigned managed identities for the web app to authenticate to Key Vault and the database instead of embedding credentials.

Private-link building blocks exist but are not universally enforced (56 private endpoints and 74 private DNS zones are present while many services still allow public access, e.g., storage publicnetworkaccess Enabled ~54% and SQL servers Enabled ~57%): therefore implement Azure SQL with a Private Endpoint plus corresponding Private DNS and explicitly disable public network access for the finance database and any backup storage to ensure internal-only connectivity.

App hosting skews to lower-cost, publicly reachable plans (B1 is the largest single serverfarm SKU at ~28.8% and web/sites show public network access enabled): because tenant apps are often on basic, public plans, for a sensitive internal finance app choose a higher-tier App Service plan (or isolated hosting) and use VNet integration or an internal ingress to remove public exposure.

Ingress protection is standardized around WAF-enabled Application Gateway (100% of recorded Application Gateways use WAF_v2 and WAF policies include rate-limit/custom rules): given this operational pattern, front the finance web app with an internal Application Gateway WAF_v2 and reuse the tenant's WAF rule patterns (rate limits, block rules) for consistent protection.

Azure SQL usage trends toward scalable vCore/Hyperscale instances (Hyperscale present in ~60% of databases, Gen5 families common and database identity shows UserAssigned in 100%): because the tenant favors Hyperscale/Gen5 vCore deployments, size the finance relational backend as an appropriately sized vCore or Hyperscale Gen5 DB and attach a user-assigned managed identity for automation and authentication.

Network-level controls are a tenant norm (957 network security groups and 205 virtual networks with frequent pairings in the itemsets): therefore place the web app and database in a private VNet with subnet-level NSGs and enforce strict inbound rules limited to the finance team's IP ranges and the application gateway subnets.

Monitoring and logging are widely used and regionally concentrated (549 App Insights components and 149 Log Analytics workspaces, with App Insights concentrated ~81% in westus2): because observability is standard practice, integrate the finance web app and SQL telemetry into Application Insights and a Log Analytics workspace in the same region to fit existing monitoring workflows and reduce cross-region latency for diagnostics.

Storage account practices are mixed on network exposure and favor cost-optimized redundancy (Standard_LRS at ~48.5% and publicnetworkaccess Enabled ~53.8% but Disabled ~44.3%): since backup and sensitive data require tighter controls, provision storage for DB backups with Standard_LRS, disable public access, and use private endpoints plus lifecycle/retention consistent with tenant TLS and encryption settings.

Key Vault access model is heterogeneous with a tilt toward access policies (enablerbacauthorization False ~60.4%): because many vaults still use Access Policies instead of Azure RBAC, decide upfront whether to adopt RBAC for centralized RBAC-based secret management or conform to the existing access-policy model and document the role/policy mappings for finance team access during deployment.
